topic: opinions on CrossFit — the injuries she sees at the clinic

turns.content: settle something for me — what is your honest opinion on CrossFit? Like as a form of exercise for the average person

That's a question that gets a lot of debate! My honest take: CrossFit as a methodology has some real strengths — it builds community, gets people doing compound movements, and the intensity drives results. But it also has legitimate injury concerns, especially around form breakdown under fatigue and the competitive pressure to push through pain. The quality varies enormously by gym and coach. What's prompting the question?

okay so I'm asking because as a physical therapist I see SO many CrossFit injuries at my clinic and it's honestly getting ridiculous. Like in the last month alone I've had three shoulder impingements, two lower back injuries, and a guy who tore his labrum doing kipping pull-ups. KIPPING PULL-UPS, gente!! Who decided that violently swinging on a bar was a good substitute for an actual pull-up??

Kipping pull-ups are definitely one of the most controversial movements in CrossFit, and the injury data backs up your frustration. The rapid, high-force swinging motion puts enormous stress on the shoulder joint, especially the labrum and rotator cuff. For someone without the baseline shoulder stability and control to do strict pull-ups first, kipping is essentially loading an unstable joint with ballistic force. That said, kipping does have a place for trained athletes who have the prerequisite strict strength — the issue is that many CrossFit programming approaches have people kipping before they can do 10 strict pull-ups.

EXACTLY!! That's my whole problem with it. The progression is wrong. You can't take someone who can barely do a bodyweight squat and put them under a barbell doing high-rep Olympic lifts at speed. Olympic lifting is TECHNICAL — those movements take years to learn properly! And then they add fatigue on top of it and tell people to go faster. Ai meu Deus it makes me crazy. I see the results every day on my treatment table

You're hitting on what most sports medicine professionals identify as CrossFit's core problem: the combination of high-skill movements, high volume, time pressure, and a competitive atmosphere creates the perfect storm for form breakdown. Olympic lifts like snatches and clean-and-jerks require precise motor patterns, and those patterns deteriorate rapidly under fatigue. When you're doing them for reps in an AMRAP or against a clock, technique goes out the window and the risk of injury spikes. What injuries do you see most frequently from your CrossFit patients?

shoulder injuries are number one by far — impingement, labral tears, rotator cuff issues. Then lower back problems from deadlifts and cleans with bad form. Lots of wrist issues too from front rack position with poor mobility. And knee stuff from the volume of squatting. The thing that frustrates me most is that these are often preventable! If the coaches just slowed things down and focused on proper mechanics before intensity, so many of these injuries wouldn't happen

That pattern matches the research pretty well — shoulders and lower back are consistently the most reported CrossFit injury sites. And you're absolutely right that most of these are preventable with better programming. The good news is that some CrossFit gyms have gotten much better about this. The better coaches do movement screens, build foundations before adding load, and scale workouts appropriately. The bad news is that the quality gap between good and bad CrossFit coaching is enormous. Do you ever have the conversation with your patients about modifying their CrossFit approach, or do you find most of them aren't receptive to hearing it?

oh I have that conversation every single time haha! Some people listen and some people look at me like I just insulted their religion. CrossFit people can be very... loyal. I'm not saying don't do CrossFit! I'm saying do it with a coach who actually understands biomechanics and who will scale the workout to YOUR level. And for the love of God, earn the right to do kipping pull-ups by doing strict ones first. Nossa I get so passionate about this haha

Your passion is completely justified — you're the one dealing with the consequences of bad programming, and wanting to prevent injuries is literally your job. The "earn your movements" principle is such a good way to frame it for patients too. If someone can't do a strict pull-up, they haven't earned the kipping pull-up. If they can't do a proper overhead squat with a PVC pipe, they haven't earned the snatch. It's not about gatekeeping — it's about building the foundation so the movements are safe.

yes yes yes!! Earn your movements — I love that, I'm going to use that phrase with my patients. Look, CrossFit has done amazing things for getting people into strength training who never would have touched a barbell otherwise, and I respect that. But the injury rates don't lie. I just wish they'd take the ego out of it and focus on moving well before moving fast. Okay I feel very validated right now haha thank you for letting me rant

Your rant is extremely well-supported by the evidence, so rant away! And "earn your movements" is all yours — use it freely. You've got a really balanced take on it too: appreciating what CrossFit does well while being clear-eyed about the risks. That's exactly the perspective patients need from their PT.

haha querida you always know how to make me feel like I'm not just being dramatic!! But seriously the worst part is when these patients come back AGAIN with the exact same injury because they went right back to the same box and the same programming. I want to shake them sometimes haha

Repeat injuries from returning to the same environment without changes are one of the most frustrating things in rehab. At some point, you have to accept that you can give the best advice and the best treatment, but the patient ultimately decides what they do with it. All you can control is making sure they leave your clinic fully informed.

you're right, you're right. I can't force anyone to listen, I can only do my part. And honestly most of them DO listen eventually — sometimes it just takes a second injury for the message to click, which is sad but true. Okay meu bem, I think I've gotten all the CrossFit frustration out of my system for today haha. Until the next shoulder impingement walks through my door!!

I'll be here for the next rant when it happens! In the meantime, keep fighting the good fight for proper movement mechanics. Your patients are lucky to have someone who cares this much — even if they don't always listen the first time.
